content:
Context file: ppt-collaboration-context
Description: Session-local working memory for collaborative PPT/HTML presentation creation. Update whenever the user gives style preferences, content decisions, rejected directions, or workflow changes.
Last updated by: agent
Reason: User added WeChat delivery rule for PDF-to-group-meeting-PPT tasks.

PPT collaboration protocol:
- Treat PPT creation as iterative co-design, not one-shot generation.
- During discussion, persist user preferences, rejected styles, target audience, purpose, structure, tone, examples, and feedback in this file.
- Prefer showing visual options over asking abstract style questions.
- Default final deliverable should be PowerPoint/PPTX unless the user explicitly asks for HTML.
- HTML can still be used as an optional preview/prototyping format when helpful, but should not be assumed as the final output.
- Keep every slide viewport-safe: no scrolling, content split instead of crammed, responsive to common 16:9 presentation screens.
- Avoid generic AI-looking slide aesthetics; choose a distinctive visual language fitted to the topic and audience.
- Do not summarize after every discussion turn. Keep replies focused; summarize only when the solution/workflow is settled or when the user asks.
- If a request arrives through WeChat asking to create a group-meeting/lab-meeting academic report PPT from a PDF, follow the PDF-to-PPTX workflow, generate the PPTX, then send the completed PPTX file back to the user through the WeChat reply-file tool when an event context is available.

Primary PPT use case:
- User mainly creates academic literature presentation decks.
- Purpose: read a paper and produce a concise, standardized, polished academic report.
- Final output should be editable PPTX.
- Style should be academic, clean, restrained, natural, and visually attractive.
- Slides should be figure-first: paper figures/data panels are the main evidence and narrative anchors.
- Text should be minimal; no dense paragraphs; use short labels, figure callouts, concise takeaways, and small captions.
- Use icons sparingly as visual anchors to improve navigation and emphasis.
- Include the research lab logo/identity. Current logo path: `/Users/lose/Downloads/网页环境/HAOLAB 2/HAOLAB-师大红版.png`. Logo is dark red on white, large emblem with HAOLAB text; likely suitable for title/footer branding and red accent palette.

Preferred academic literature deck structure:
- Slide 1: Paper title, journal information, impact factor, citation count, and key bibliographic metadata.
- Slide 2: Author information, including author academic career, major affiliations, biography, and research trajectory.
- Later slides: paper background, scientific question, experimental data, key results, and interpretation.
- Data/result slides should be built around extracted paper figures and panels.
- Maintain a dedicated material/assets folder for paper images and supporting visuals.

Potential asset workflow:
- Create/use one folder per paper deck for figures, author photos, journal screenshots/logos if appropriate, lab logo, and extracted diagrams.
- Prefer meaningful filenames for assets, e.g. `fig1_overview.png`, `fig2_model.png`, `author_corresponding.png`, `journal_cover.png`.
- Agent should help inventory assets, identify which figure/panel belongs to which slide, and avoid using low-quality/cropped images unless needed.

Skill exploration notes:
- Existing local `frontend-slides` skill is useful for co-design habits, style preview, viewport safety, and HTML slide generation, but it defaults to HTML and is not enough for the user's PPTX-first workflow.
- `dom-to-pptx` ships `dom-to-pptx-skill` via `npx dom-to-pptx-skills`. Its skill targets high-fidelity editable PowerPoint through HTML-to-PPTX conversion. It includes safe HTML template, CSS whitelist, validation script, sample layout prompts, and style presets.
- `dom-to-pptx-skill` is not directly ideal for this user: its default language is premium business/atmospheric UI, bento/glassmorphism, internet imagery, and browser export. It should be adapted into an academic literature-report skill with figure-first layouts, restrained scientific typography, paper figure callouts, citations, author/journal metadata, HAOLAB branding, and material folder discipline.
- No existing local skill appears specifically built for concise academic literature PPTX reports. Creating a custom opencode skill may be worthwhile after the workflow stabilizes.

Current known user intent:
- The user wants to explore a better PPT-making workflow by updating session-local files during the discussion.
- The user prefers PPT/PPTX over HTML as the primary output format.
- The user is interested in whether an MCP can directly operate PPTX/PowerPoint, similar to live-mcp-word.
- No specific paper, audience detail, target slide count, or final visual template has been chosen yet.

MCP/PPTX exploration notes:
- `mcp-powerpoint` exists. It provides basic MCP/CLI operations: create presentation, add slide, get slides, read metadata, export PDF. It wraps `pptxgenjs`/`officegen`, so it is closer to file generation than live editing. README notes existing slide editing limitations and simulated PDF export.
- `local-mcp` exists and advertises local macOS/Windows app access, including Documents tools. README lists `ppt_create` and `ppt_read`, but does not show fine-grained slide editing tools. It may be useful for simple create/read, not necessarily robust PPT authoring.
- `@mindstone-engineering/mcp-server-office` is the most relevant live-Office-style MCP found so far. It bundles a stdio MCP server, local HTTPS sidecar, and Office Add-in assets. It bridges MCP to an Office task pane over WebSocket and calls Office.js inside Word/Excel/PowerPoint. README says PowerPoint has 12 tools: slide manipulation, text/shape/image insert, master/layout reads, notes, export, etc. Requires Node 20+, Microsoft 365 desktop, macOS/Windows, add-in sideload/certs. License is FSL-1.1-MIT converting to MIT in 2030. Needs practical setup/test before adopting.
- `@neuraforge/office-mcp` is a file-level Office MCP with 32 tools. PowerPoint tools include create/read/list slides, add/delete/duplicate slides, modify slide, find/replace text, update table cell, and read detailed presentation structure. It depends on `pptxgenjs`, `jszip`, etc. It likely edits files, not live PowerPoint. Easier to run than an add-in but probably less design/control fidelity than custom code.

PPT tool/library exploration notes:
- `pptxgenjs` is the baseline JS PPTX generation library. It creates editable PPTX with text, tables, shapes, images, charts, slide masters, SVG, animated GIFs, Asian fonts, and browser/Node support. Strong for creating decks from scratch; less ideal for editing complex existing decks.
- `pptx-automizer` is strong for template-based PPTX generation and modification. It can import existing .pptx templates, merge slides, modify named/creationId shapes, replace text, swap images, modify tables/charts, remove elements, and use PptxGenJS for generated objects. Good fit if user provides polished templates. Limitations: server-side only, animations out of scope/problematic, not direct in-place editing; output is rebuilt from loaded templates.
- `dom-to-pptx` converts HTML DOM to fully editable PowerPoint slides, not screenshots. It maps computed layout/styles to native PPT shapes/text and advertises gradients, shadows, rounded images, font embedding, multi-slide export, SVG vector support, and an AI-agent skill installer via `npx dom-to-pptx-skills`. Potentially promising for visual design iteration while preserving editability, but needs local test.
- `@helping-ai-workflow/slide-to-pptx` converts `open-slide` React decks to editable PPTX using Playwright measurement plus PptxGenJS and XML post-processing. It outputs native shapes/text/groups, not rasterized images. Strong if adopting open-slide primitives; less general because it expects an open-slide project structure.
- `pptx-svg` is a PPTX-SVG round-trip library with shape/text/image/slide editing APIs and export back to PPTX. It supports rendering slides as SVG, updating shape transforms/text/fills/strokes, adding/deleting/duplicating shapes, slide add/delete/reorder, image replacement, notes/comments preservation. Very interesting as a possible custom MCP backend for existing PPTX inspection/editing. Requires deeper testing.
- `pptx-glimpse` renders PPTX to SVG/PNG in Node without LibreOffice. Useful for preview, thumbnail generation, and giving vision models slide images for review. It is not primarily an authoring library.

Potential workflow hypothesis, not final:
- For quickest reliable PPTX generation: use `pptxgenjs` directly or via a custom session/project tool.
- For template-driven professional decks: use `pptx-automizer` with user-provided template slides and named placeholders.
- For high-visual editable output: prototype in HTML/CSS and convert with `dom-to-pptx`, then inspect/adjust.
- For existing PPTX inspection/render/review: combine `pptx-svg` or `pptx-glimpse` with a custom tool.
- For live PowerPoint control: test `@mindstone-engineering/mcp-server-office` first; it is the closest match to live-mcp-word style but setup complexity is higher.
